metric_value: Nearly three-fold growth from $7.2M (1997) to $20.7M (2000)
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: 75 percent decline from $20.7M (2000) to $5.1M (2004) over four years
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: More than $9.6 million in accumulated net losses from 2001 through 2004
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: Nearly 37 percent net profit margin on Jan-Aug 2006 revenue
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation
notes: Kastner notes this profitability profile made the acquisition attractive

metric_value: Multi-layered cooperative revenue-sharing replaced by market-rate salaries and conventional bonus plans; model could not scale beyond ~12 analysts
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: Cannot administer complex revenue splits across 100 analysts; feasible across 12.
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: Supply chain management, CRM, human capital management, vertical industry analysis (financial services, telecom, retail, manufacturing, healthcare)
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: Board asked Kastner to step aside from 2003 to 2006 while Bedard restructured business model; Kastner kept Intel as retainer
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_name: Kastner returned to Aberdeen 2006 for SOA research
metric_value: Returned in 2006 to drive SOA research, contributing to 2005-2006 revenue and profitability recovery
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: Systematic surveys of hundreds of enterprises on technology adoption patterns, deployment challenges, and satisfaction metrics
source_page: Enhanced Research Practices

metric_value: CIOs used Aberdeen benchmarks to justify budget decisions; vendors quoted them in sales materials and challenged assumptions when numbers didn't favor them.
source_page: Enhanced Research Practices

metric_value: Captured architectural decisions, org structures, vendor selections, and PM approaches from successful deployments as reusable frameworks
source_page: Enhanced Research Practices
notes: Kastner describes this as 'knowledge management before that term became fashionable'

metric_name: TCO modeling as signature commercial tool
metric_value: TCO calculators for major technology categories became Aberdeen's most commercially potent tool; incorporated hardware, software, implementation, maintenance, training over 3-5 year horizons
source_page: Enhanced Research Practices

metric_value: TCO models positioned Aberdeen's advisory work as quantitatively rigorous rather than qualitatively opinionated; both vendor challenge and endorsement proved the models were landing.
source_page: Enhanced Research Practices

metric_name: TCO vs sticker price procurement example
metric_value: Platform $1M cheaper to license might cost $3M more to implement, $2M more to staff, $1.5M more to maintain: net $5.5M more expensive over 5 years.
source_page: Sidebar: How TCO Modeling Changed Vendor Conversations

metric_value: TCO models only as good as their assumptions; vendor disagreements about TCO outcomes were usually disagreements about specific assumptions, creating valuable client conversations.
source_page: Sidebar: How TCO Modeling Changed Vendor Conversations

metric_name: Internet bubble demand for analyst validation
metric_value: Every internet startup and every enterprise digital initiative needed analyst validation; research market demand was 'irrational in the best possible direction'
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_name: Aberdeen team knew bubble was unsustainable
metric_value: Analyst team made dark jokes about which internet business models would survive; told clients directly when asked. Not everyone asked.
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_value: Knowing a bubble exists doesn't mean you can time its deflation.
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_value: NASDAQ peaked in March 2000; Aberdeen revenue peaked same year at $20.7 million
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_value: Revenue fell to $9.4M in 2002, hurt by the 9/11 attack aftermath
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_value: Headcount cut from ~150 analysts in 2001 to ~40 by 2003
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help
notes: Kastner: 'These were real people — colleagues who had built the firm through the growth years.'

metric_value: Survey-based benchmark research became less useful when response rates collapsed; companies in survival mode don't have time for analyst surveys.
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_name: Deep technical advisory durability in downturn
metric_value: Deep technical advisory work held up during downturn; enterprises still needed complex integration, architecture, and vendor selection help even with constrained budgets.
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_name: Aberdeen survival due to pre-crash profitability
metric_value: Aberdeen survived better than competitors because it maintained profitability through growth years rather than burning cash to acquire market share.
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_value: Smaller research firms were acquired or shut down; Aberdeen emerged from downturn with less competition and more realistic assessment of sustainable scale.
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_name: SOA as decade-defining architectural shift prediction
metric_value: By 2000 Kastner argued SOA represented the decade's defining architectural shift, comparable to client-server in the 1990s; applications would decompose into loosely coupled services.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — SOA
notes: Thesis proved correct; timeline was optimistic; standards (SOAP, WSDL, UDDI) were immature

metric_value: By 2004-2005 SOA reached peak hype; IBM, Oracle, BEA, Microsoft, SAP promoted SOA platforms; many enterprise SOA initiatives delivered disappointing results.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — SOA

metric_value: By 2008-2010, REST APIs and lightweight service approaches displaced heavyweight SOAP-based SOA while preserving architectural principles of loose coupling.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — SOA
notes: Kastner calls this 'a successful prediction with an imprecise timestamp'

metric_value: Predicted early 2000s that open source would move from internet infrastructure (Linux, Apache, MySQL) into enterprise applications and middleware.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Open Source
notes: Adoption played out as predicted; took full decade rather than initially projected 2-3 years

metric_value: No license fees, transparent code for security audit, community-driven innovation were compelling economic advantages for enterprise open source adoption.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Open Source

metric_value: Resistance to open source was less from technical concerns than from procurement processes; enterprise IT accustomed to paying vendors for software and bundled support contracts.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Open Source

metric_name: Data integration as perpetual tax prediction
metric_value: Integration complexity would accelerate despite improved tools; each new application, acquired company, or data source adds to integration burden (N-squared complexity).
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Data Integration
notes: Kastner states this 'remains accurate in 2026'

metric_value: Integration technology market exploded 2000-2006 with EAI platforms, ETL tools, data quality software, MDM systems, and SOA infrastructure; integration remained expensive because application proliferation outpaced tools.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Data Integration

metric_value: By 2004-2005 predicted consumer technology would increasingly influence enterprise IT; employees would demand consumer-grade UX from enterprise systems.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Consumerization of IT
notes: Mainstream discussion in CIO circles by 2007-2008

metric_value: Salesforce.com embodied consumerization of IT: consumer-grade interfaces and elimination of complex installation challenged traditional enterprise software vendors.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Consumerization of IT

metric_value: Smartphones were still BlackBerry keyboards in 2005, not iPhone touchscreens, but the direction toward consumerization was visible.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Consumerization of IT

metric_value: Jamie Bedard became Aberdeen Group CEO in mid 2002; priorities: operational excellence, research productization, sales force professionalization, financial discipline.
source_page: The CEO Transition: Jamie Bedard

metric_value: Tom Willmott was Aberdeen's effective operational leader through partnership era and early growth phase; by 2003, scaling to 150 employees required different capabilities.
source_page: The CEO Transition: Jamie Bedard

metric_value: Tom Willmott remained in strategic advisory capacity after the Bedard CEO transition; founding partners maintained significant equity and leadership roles.
source_page: The CEO Transition: Jamie Bedard

metric_value: Bedard's style more corporate and less collegial than partnership era; created friction with analysts but provided stability and predictability the scaled organization required.
source_page: The CEO Transition: Jamie Bedard

metric_value: Kastner provided input on research strategy and major client engagements; Bedard focused on operational management and board relations.
source_page: The CEO Transition: Jamie Bedard

metric_name: Founder vs professional CEO skill set distinction
metric_value: Founders exceptional at research, client advisory, domain expertise, cooperative relationship management — not the same skills as managing 150-person org through downturn with venture investors.
source_page: Sidebar: What 'Professional CEO' Transition Really Means

metric_name: Institutional professionalism vs early-stage culture trade-off
metric_value: Institutional professionalism creates discipline and scalability at the cost of informal energy, personal accountability, and cultural distinctiveness. Cannot have both at scale.
source_page: Sidebar: What 'Professional CEO' Transition Really Means

metric_value: Harte-Hanks approached Aberdeen in early 2006 to participate in sale process; Aberdeen's sale was an auction format.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_value: Stated thesis: Aberdeen research could enhance Harte-Hanks consulting; Harte-Hanks sales force could expand Aberdeen's customer base. Harte-Hanks owned database of 4,500 US IT decision-makers.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_value: Aberdeen served CIOs, infrastructure architects, dev managers. Harte-Hanks served CMOs/marketing directors. Customer segments overlapped minimally. Business cases more adjacent than complementary.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_value: $42 million acquisition valuation; closed October 2006
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_name: Acquisition timing luck: financial crisis avoided
metric_value: A year after October 2006 close, financial crisis erupted and M&A markets froze; transaction would not have happened at that valuation — or any valuation — twelve months later.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_value: Cross-selling mandates attempted to sell Aberdeen research to Harte-Hanks marketing clients and vice versa; customer segment mismatch never accepted.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_value: Harte-Hanks wanted Aberdeen research repurposed as lead generation content for consulting services; shifted output from independent analysis toward marketing-oriented content.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_value: Key analysts departed as Aberdeen's culture changed under Harte-Hanks ownership.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_value: By 2010-2012, almost all Aberdeen published output was vendor-sponsored survey-based reports — content marketing rather than independent research.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_value: Aberdeen brand now part of Ziff-Davis as of the time of Kastner's writing (2026).
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_name: Kastner departure from Aberdeen: nine SOA studies
metric_value: Kastner departed mid-2007 after completing nine SOA studies in nine months; after 18 years building and leading the research practice.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_name: Scale enables capabilities but dilutes culture
metric_value: Firm could do more at 150 analysts than at 30; but intimate collaboration and technical depth of partnership era harder to maintain; cooperative revenue-sharing couldn't survive corporate compensation.
source_page: What the Go-Go Years Proved

metric_value: Venture investment enabled expansion but created pressure for revenue growth and eventual exit; sustainable profitability mindset gave way to growth-oriented thinking culminating in acquisition.
source_page: What the Go-Go Years Proved

metric_name: Market cycles are brutal and non-negotiable
metric_value: 2000-2004 downturn: revenue fell 75% from peak; accumulated losses exceeded $9.6M over four years; overextended firms failed; Aberdeen survived with lasting humility about adoption timelines.
source_page: What the Go-Go Years Proved

metric_value: Technically deep architecture and implementation advisory retained premium pricing throughout every market cycle; market sizing and trend analysis were commoditizing.
source_page: What the Go-Go Years Proved

metric_value: Closed October 2006 at $42M; financial crisis arrived 2007. Skilled negotiation and preparation set up the transaction. Fortune closed it.
source_page: What the Go-Go Years Proved

metric_name: Partnership vs scaled firm cultural comparison
metric_value: Aberdeen 1990-1997 as partnership was more culturally distinctive and professionally interesting; scaled Aberdeen 1998-2006 created far more wealth. These tensions are fundamental to every entrepreneurial venture.
source_page: What the Go-Go Years Proved

metric_value: Aberdeen founded ~1988 (Kastner references '18 years building and leading the research practice' as of mid-2007 departure; also references 1990-1997 as partnership era)
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition
notes: Date inferred from '18 years' and 2007 departure; founding as partnership referenced as circa 1990

metric_name: SOA standards immaturity at adoption time
metric_value: SOAP, WSDL, UDDI standards were immature during early SOA adoption window; organizational governance discipline didn't yet exist in most enterprises.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — SOA

metric_name: JBoss, MySQL, PostgreSQL, LAMP enterprise penetration
metric_value: JBoss, MySQL, PostgreSQL, and eventually full LAMP stacks penetrated enterprise infrastructure across 2000-2006
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Open Source

metric_name: N-squared integration complexity is mathematical property
metric_value: Integration complexity is N-squared: each new application, acquired company, or data source multiplies integration burden. This is a mathematical property, not a solvable engineering problem.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Data Integration

metric_value: Despite better tools, integration remained expensive because application proliferation outpaced integration technology improvement through 2000-2006.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Data Integration

metric_value: Technology spending during 1998-2001 had disconnected from economic reality; enterprises invested in e-commerce, web platforms, integration with optimistic ROI assumptions that couldn't survive scrutiny.
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_value: Harte-Hanks owned a database of 4,500 US IT decision-makers intended to complement Aberdeen's research distribution
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_value: Aberdeen's sale was a multi-tier auction; Kastner believes auction format boosted interest and participation
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_name: Financial logic trumped strategic skepticism in acquisition
metric_value: Harte-Hanks willing to pay substantial premium; Aberdeen stakeholders (founders, VCs, management) ready to monetize after sixteen years. Financial logic trumped strategic skepticism.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_name: Venture capital enabled new coverage domains
metric_value: Aberdeen at scale could cover supply chain management, CRM, human capital management, vertical industries — problems Aberdeen as a four-partner firm could not cover.
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: Kastner's role shifted from direct client delivery to research strategy, analyst development, and competitive positioning of firm's capabilities as Aberdeen scaled.
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: The transition from practitioner to leader — and back again — is a standard arc in professional services. That doesn't make it comfortable either time.
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_name: Hard problems don't pause for recessions
metric_value: Enterprises still needed to solve complex integration, architecture, and vendor selection problems even with constrained budgets. The hard problems don't pause for recessions.
source_page: The Internet Bubble: We Knew It, and It Didn't Help

metric_name: Market cycle humility about adoption timeline predictions
metric_value: Post-crash experience produced lasting humility about predicting technology adoption timelines; Kastner notes SOA and open source timelines were optimistic by 2-3 years.
source_page: What the Go-Go Years Proved

metric_name: Salesforce eliminated complex installation as differentiator
metric_value: Salesforce.com offered consumer-grade interfaces and eliminated complex installation, challenging traditional enterprise software vendors designed for trained power users.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Consumerization of IT

metric_name: Mobile revolution prediction from consumerization thesis
metric_value: Kastner predicted coming mobile revolution; smartphones still BlackBerry keyboards in 2005 but direction was visible; by 2007-2008 consumerization of IT mainstream in CIO circles.
source_page: The Predictions That Defined the Era — Consumerization of IT

metric_name: Aberdeen expansion: office space and analyst recruiting
metric_value: Following venture capital infusion, office space expanded and analyst recruiting accelerated immediately
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: A subscription revenue model was built alongside the advisory practice following the VC infusion
source_page: The Transformation: From Partnership to Corporation

metric_value: Aberdeen stakeholders ready to monetize after sixteen years at October 2006 acquisition close
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_value: By mid-2007, the Aberdeen Kastner co-founded no longer existed in any meaningful sense. Brand persisted; substance absorbed into a different kind of company with different priorities.
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition

metric_name: TCO analysis shifted vendor conversation from sticker price to 5-year economics
metric_value: TCO analysis created a defensible methodology for choosing on five-year economics rather than initial price; positioned Aberdeen's advisory as quantitatively rigorous.
source_page: Sidebar: How TCO Modeling Changed Vendor Conversations

metric_value: Aberdeen was rigorous about documenting and making assumptions visible; conversations about assumptions were often more valuable to clients than the final number.
source_page: Sidebar: How TCO Modeling Changed Vendor Conversations

metric_name: Best practices as knowledge management before the term
metric_value: Best practices frameworks represented knowledge management before that term became fashionable — systematic capture and reuse of insights across a larger analyst population.
source_page: Enhanced Research Practices

metric_value: Vendors who fared poorly in Aberdeen models challenged assumptions; vendors who fared well quoted Aberdeen in sales decks. Both behaviors proved the models were landing.
source_page: Enhanced Research Practices

metric_value: Kastner built and led Aberdeen's research practice for 18 years before departing mid-2007
source_page: The Harte-Hanks Acquisition
